2026-02-27

River systems as networks

  • River systems can be represented as graphs/networks
  • Nodes (vertices) represent river segments
  • Links (edges) represent the connections (confluences and barriers) between segments
  • Many connectivity indices are defined for graphs, straightforward application to river systems when represented as graphs
  • Inclusion of barriers complicates the representation, but several indices can be adapted to account for them (e.g. DCI, Dendritic Connectivity Index)

The topology of river networks

  • Planar networks: no links (edges) intersections
  • Tree-like networks: no loops, only one path between any two nodes (vertices)
  • Number of nodes (vertices) and links (edges) in a tree-like network: E = V - 1
  • Weighted networks: node weights can represent attributes of river segments (e.g. length, discharge, etc.), edge weights can represent the strength of the connection (e.g. presence/absence of a barrier, etc.)

What we will cover in this presentation

  • The igraph package for graph manipulation in R
  • Processing of geographic data (e.g. HydroRIVERS, HydroBASINS, HydroATLAS datasets) to generate river networks without dams
  • processing of geographic data (e.g. AMBER) to generate river networks with dams
  • Most of the information is covered already in the riverconn tutorial

Which packages we will use

  • sf and terra for vector and raster data manipulation

  • dplyr for data manipulation: helps building pipelines for easy-to-read data manipulation (loaded with the tidyverse)

  • ggplot2 for data visualization: a bit painful, but easily integrates sf and terra objects for plotting (loaded with the tidyverse)

  • igraph for graph manipulation: useful for building/editing/managing graphs

Which packages we will use

library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(terra)
library(tidyterra)
library(viridis)
library(igraph)

The igraph package

The igraph package

  • The igraph package implements routines for graphs and network analysis.
  • It can handle large graphs very well and provides functions for generating random and regular graphs, graph visualization, centrality methods and much more.
  • The packages allows for easy construction of igraph objects based on symbolic description of edges and vertices, edges and vertices lists, or adjacency matrices.
  • The book Statistical Analysis of Network Data with R by Kolaczyk and Csardi (2014) offers a comprehensive tutorial on the possibilities offered by the igraph package, including network data manipulation and visualization, descriptive analysis of network graphs, mathematical and statistical modeling of graphs, and network topology inference.

The igraph package

We build a graph using a symbolic representation of vertices and edges. The notation 1–2 means that there is an edge between vertices 1 and 2.

g <- igraph::graph_from_literal(1--2, 2--5, 3--4, 4--5, 6--7)
plot(g)

The igraph package

We check the graph. The graph has 7 vertices and 5 edged and is undirected (UN). The graph has the attribute name, that is a vertex attribute of class character. The graph has no edge attributes.

g
## IGRAPH 0ba6ffe UN-- 7 5 -- 
## + attr: name (v/c)
## + edges from 0ba6ffe (vertex names):
## [1] 1--2 2--5 5--4 3--4 6--7

The igraph package

We build a directed graph using a symbolic representation of vertices and edges. The notation 1-+2 means that there is an edge with direction from 1 and 2.

g <- igraph::graph_from_literal(1-+2, 2-+5, 3-+4, 4-+5, 6-+7)
plot(g)

The igraph package

We check the graph. The graph has 7 vertices and 5 edged and is directed (DN). The graph has the attribute name, that is a vertex attribute of class character. The graph has no edge attributes.

g
## IGRAPH 0bb09ca DN-- 7 5 -- 
## + attr: name (v/c)
## + edges from 0bb09ca (vertex names):
## [1] 1->2 2->5 3->4 4->5 6->7

The igraph package

We create a graph that is a bit more complex.

g <- igraph::graph_from_literal( 1-+2, 2-+5, 3-+4, 4-+5, 6-+7,
  7-+10, 8-+9, 9-+10, 5-+11, 11-+12, 10-+13, 13-+12, 12-+14, 14-+15, 15-+16)
plot(g)

The igraph package

We can transform this graph to an adjacenty matrix (as_adjacency_matrix(); 1 = there is an edge). Note that the matrix is not symmetric because the graph is directed.

mat <- igraph::as_adjacency_matrix(g)
mat[1:8, 1:8]
## 8 x 8 sparse Matrix of class "dgCMatrix"
##    1 2 5 3 4 6 7 10
## 1  . 1 . . . . .  .
## 2  . . 1 . . . .  .
## 5  . . . . . . .  .
## 3  . . . . 1 . .  .
## 4  . . 1 . . . .  .
## 6  . . . . . . 1  .
## 7  . . . . . . .  1
## 10 . . . . . . .  .

The igraph package

The graph can be generated back from the adjacency matrix (graph_from_adjacency_matrix()). We check if the new graph is isomorphic (isomorphic function) to the original one (i.e. they are the same graph, but with different vertex labels).

g1 <- igraph::graph_from_adjacency_matrix(mat)
igraph::isomorphic(g1, g)
## [1] TRUE

The igraph package

We can transform this graph to a data.frame (as_data_frame()). Note that the data.frame has two columns, from and to, that represent the source and target vertices of each edge.

g_df <- igraph::as_data_frame(g, "edges")
head(g_df)
##   from to
## 1    1  2
## 2    2  5
## 3    5 11
## 4    3  4
## 5    4  5
## 6    6  7

The igraph package

The graph can be generated back from the data.frame (graph_from_data_frame()). We check if the new graph is isomorphic (isomorphic) to the original one (i.e. they are the same graph, but with different vertex labels).

g1 <- igraph::graph_from_data_frame(g_df)
igraph::isomorphic(g1, g)
## [1] TRUE

Centrality metrics with the igraph package

Centrality metrics with the igraph package

  • Centrality metrics are used to identify the most important vertices in a graph
  • Identify nodes that are relevant for connecting separate sections of the graph
  • Degree centrality: number of edges connected to a vertex (for weighted networks: strenght)
  • Betweenness centrality: number of shortest paths that pass through a vertex
  • Closeness (harmonic) centrality: inverse of the average shortest path length from a vertex
  • For directed and undirected graphs

Centrality metrics with the igraph package

Degree centrality of node \(i\) (\(k_{i}\)): number of edges connected to a vertex (for weighted networks: strenght)

\[k_i = \sum_{j}a_{ij}\] where \(a_{ij}\) is the element of the adjacency matrix that represents the presence (1) or absence (0) of an edge between vertices \(i\) and \(j\)

Centrality metrics with the igraph package

Betweenness centrality: number of shortest paths that pass through a vertex

\[g_i = \sum_{s \neq i \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}} \] where where \(\sigma_{st}\) is the total number of shortest paths from node \(s\) to node \(t\) and \(\sigma_{st}(i)\) is the number of those paths that pass through node \(i\) (not where \(i\) is an end point)

Centrality metrics with the igraph package

Closeness centrality: inverse of the average shortest path length from a vertex

\[c_i = \frac{1}{\sum_{j \neq i} {d(ij)}} \] Harmonic centrality: for disconnected graphs (to avoid issues with \(d(ij) = \infty\))

\[c_i = \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{1}{ {d(ij)}} \]

where \(d(ij)\) is the shortest distance between nodes \(i\) and \(j\)

Centrality metrics with the igraph package

We create a graph, calculate degree, betweeness and closeness centrality and store the values as nodes attributes

g <- igraph::graph_from_literal( 1-+2, 2-+5, 3-+4, 4-+5, 6-+7,
  7-+10, 8-+9, 9-+10, 5-+11, 11-+12, 10-+13, 13-+12, 12-+14, 14-+15, 15-+16)
V(g)$degree <- igraph::degree(g)
V(g)$betweenness <- igraph::betweenness(g)
V(g)$closeness <- igraph::closeness(g, mode = "all")
g
## IGRAPH 0c01159 DN-- 16 15 -- 
## + attr: name (v/c), degree (v/n), betweenness (v/n), closeness (v/n)
## + edges from 0c01159 (vertex names):
##  [1] 1 ->2  2 ->5  5 ->11 3 ->4  4 ->5  6 ->7  7 ->10 10->13 8 ->9  9 ->10
## [11] 11->12 12->14 13->12 14->15 15->16

Centrality metrics with the igraph package

We plot the graph with vertex color proportional to the centrality metrics

library(ggnetwork)
gg0 <- ggnetwork(g)

pl1 <- ggplot(gg0, aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend)) +
  geom_edges(arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.5, "lines"), type = "closed")) + 
  geom_nodes(aes(color = degree), size = 5) + theme_void()

pl2 <- ggplot(gg0, aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend)) +
  geom_edges(arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.5, "lines"), type = "closed")) + 
  geom_nodes(aes(color = closeness), size = 5) + theme_void()

pl3 <- ggplot(gg0, aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend)) +
  geom_edges(arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.5, "lines"), type = "closed")) + 
  geom_nodes(aes(color = betweenness), size = 5) + theme_void()

plot <- ggpubr::ggarrange(pl1, pl2, pl3, ncol = 3, nrow = 1)

Centrality metrics with the igraph package

The Ebro case study

The Ebro case study

We load the input data in the environment. We have three shapefiles: one for the river network, one for the river basin and one for the dams. The dams are from a local dataset, that contains also informations on the upstream reservoir.

shape_river <- "data/HydroRIVERS_sample/sample__hydroRIVERS_v10_eu.shp" %>% 
  st_read(quiet = TRUE)
shape_basin <- "data/HydroBASINS_sample/sample__hybas_eu_lev10_v1c.shp" %>% 
  st_read(quiet = TRUE)
shape_dams <- "data/dams_ebro/embalses.shp" %>% 
  st_read(quiet = TRUE)

Then, we load the content of the file functions_network_creation.R, that contains several useful functions.

source("functions_network_creation.R")

The Ebro case study

The river shapefile has a very high resolution. To speed up the analyses, we drop all the river segments that have an upland area greater than 80 km2.

shape_river_small <- shape_river %>% 
  filter(UPLAND_SKM > 80)

nrow(shape_river)
## [1] 4980
nrow(shape_river_small)
## [1] 1870

The Ebro case study

We convert the dams shapefile from polygons to points (the polygons are the borders of the reservoir).

dams_to_points <- shape_dams %>%
  st_as_sf() %>% st_centroid() %>%
  mutate(id = 1:nrow(.)) %>%  dplyr::select(id) %>% 
  st_transform(crs = "+proj=longlat +datum=WGS84")
## Warning: st_centroid assumes attributes are constant over geometries
plot <- ggplot() +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_minimal() +
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(shape_basin, fill = "white", color = "gray90") +
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(shape_river_small, aes(color = log10(UPLAND_SKM)) )+
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(dams_to_points) +
  scale_color_viridis(direction = -1, name= "upstream area \n(log10[Km^2])") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(caption = "Black dots are the position of the dams")

The Ebro case study

Hydrographic data processing

Hydrographic data processing

The first step before building the graph is to identify confluences. To do this, the river network is split into the smallest segments allowed by its geometry.

shape_river_simple <- shape_river_small %>%
  st_as_sf %>%
  st_union()

river_to_points <- shape_river_simple  %>%
  st_as_sf() %>%
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  mutate(id = 1:nrow(.))

Hydrographic data processing

Confluences are the points where three or more segments overlap.

joins_selection <- river_to_points %>%
  st_equals() %>%
  Filter(function(x){length(x) > 2}, .) %>%
  lapply(., FUN = min) %>%
  unlist() %>%
  unique()

river_joins <- river_to_points %>% 
  filter(id %in% joins_selection)

Hydrographic data processing

Finally, the simplified shapefile is split where conferences are.

shape_river_simplified <- lwgeom::st_split(shape_river_simple, river_joins) %>%
  st_collection_extract(.,"LINESTRING") %>%
  data.frame(id = 1:length(.), geometry = .) %>%
  st_as_sf() %>%
  mutate(length = st_length(.))

Hydrographic data processing

We plot to make sure everything was succesful.

plot <- ggplot() +
  coord_fixed() +
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(shape_river_simplified, aes(color = id))+
  scale_color_viridis(direction = -1, name= "Reach ID") +
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(river_joins, shape = 1)+
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")+
  labs(caption = "Hollow points are the position of the junctions")

Hydrographic data processing

Barriers data processing

Barriers data processing

We use the custom snap_to_river() function to overlay dams with the river network.

dams_snapped <- snap_to_river(dams_to_points,
                              shape_river_simple %>% st_sf(),
                              max_dist = 1000)

dams_snapped_reduced <-
  dams_snapped[
    st_contains(shape_river_simple %>% st_sf(), 
                dams_snapped, prepared = FALSE)[[1]],]

st_distance(dams_snapped_reduced, shape_river_simple) %>% sum
## 0 [m]

Barriers data processing

If two or more dams are snapped to the same point, they are considered as one dam. This does not work with

dams_snapped_reduced_joined <- dams_snapped_reduced %>%
  mutate(cluster =
           st_equals(dams_snapped_reduced, dams_snapped_reduced) %>%
           sapply(., FUN = function(x) paste0(x, collapse = "_"))) %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(id_dam = as.character(1:nrow(.)), pass_u = 0.1, pass_d = 0.8) %>%
  as.data.frame %>%
  st_as_sf() %>%
  st_join(., shape_river_small, join = st_is_within_distance, dist = 10 ) %>% 
  group_by(id) %>%
  slice(which.max(UPLAND_SKM)) %>% 
  ungroup()

Barriers data processing

The custom function headwaters_dam() is useful for identifying dams that do not have an upstream reach and would not be contributing to fragmentation.

headwaters_checking <- headwaters_dam(dams_snapped_reduced_joined, shape_river_simple)

headwaters_checking$flag_headwater %>% sum()
## [1] 0

Barriers data processing

Now dams can be plotted.

plot <- ggplot() +
  coord_fixed() +
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(shape_river_simple, color = "gray70")+
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(dams_snapped_reduced, shape = 1) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")+
  ggspatial::annotation_north_arrow(location = "br")+
  ggspatial::annotation_scale(location = "bl", style = "ticks") +
  labs(caption = "Hollow points are the position of the dams")

Barriers data processing

Integrating hydrographic and dams data

Integrating hydrographic and dams data

The position of confluences and dams are integrated in the same data.frame, that will be used to build the graph. The type of link (confluence or dam) is stored in the column type, while the columns pass_u and pass_d store the passability values for upstream and downstream movement, respectively.

network_links <- rbind(
  dams_snapped_reduced_joined %>% 
    mutate(type = "dam", id_barrier = id_dam) %>%
    dplyr::select(type, id_barrier, pass_u, pass_d),
  river_joins %>% mutate(type = "joint") %>%
    dplyr::select(type) %>%
    mutate(id_barrier = NA, pass_u = NA, pass_d = NA) %>%
    rename(geometry = x)) %>%
  mutate(id_links = 1:nrow(.))

Integrating hydrographic and dams data

Finally, the shapefile is sliced based on confluences and dams positions.

river_net_simplified <- lwgeom::st_split(shape_river_simple, network_links) %>%
  st_collection_extract(.,"LINESTRING") %>%
  data.frame(NodeID = 1:length(.), geometry = .) %>%
  st_as_sf() %>%
  mutate(length = st_length(.)) %>%
  st_join(., shape_river_small, join = st_is_within_distance, dist = 0.01 ) %>% 
  group_by(NodeID) %>%
  slice(which.max(UPLAND_SKM)) %>% 
  ungroup()

Integrating hydrographic and dams data

We check with a plot

plot <- ggplot() +
  coord_fixed() +
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(river_net_simplified, color = "gray70")+
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(network_links, aes(shape = type))+
  scale_shape_manual(name = "Splitting points", values=c("dam" =17,"joint" = 23))+
  theme_minimal() +
  ggspatial::annotation_scale(location = "bl", style = "ticks")

Integrating hydrographic and dams data

We check with a plot

Troubleshooting

Troubleshooting

A common issue when generating the river network is when more than three segments are overlapping in a confluence. The custom function multiple_confluences() returns a shapefile that flags problematic confluences.

confluences <- multiple_confluences(river_net_simplified)
confluences[1:5,]
## Simple feature collection with 5 features and 3 fields
## Geometry type: POINT
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: -4.047917 ymin: 42.81042 xmax: -1.364583 ymax: 42.97292
## Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84
## # A tibble: 5 × 4
##   NodeID             geometry n_confluences flag_confluences
##    <int>          <POINT [°]>         <int> <lgl>           
## 1      1  (-3.46875 42.95208)             3 FALSE           
## 2      2 (-4.047917 42.97292)             3 FALSE           
## 3      5 (-3.552083 42.96875)             3 FALSE           
## 4      7 (-1.364583 42.81042)             3 FALSE           
## 5     10 (-1.835417 42.90625)             3 FALSE
sum(confluences$flag_confluence)
## [1] 0

Troubleshooting

A common issue when generating the river network is when the shapefile has some gaps. The custom function multiple_confluences() returns a shapefile that flags the issue.

shp_check <- check_components(network_links, river_net_simplified)
shp_check$component %>% table()
## .
##   1 
## 646

We see that there is only one component. This is not surprising because the file was already corrected.

Troubleshooting

If the function multiple_confluences() is used on a native file, many components are found. The shapefile can be plotted to identify where the weird components are located and to correct them (e.g. deleting them or snapping them)

example_broken <- st_read("data/example_broken_river/example_broken.shp", quiet = TRUE)

shp_check <- check_components(network_links, example_broken)
shp_check$component %>% table()
## .
##   1   2   3   4   5   6 
## 644   3   1   1   1   1

Troubleshooting

We check where the shapefile is broken in a plot.

plot <- ggplot() +
  coord_fixed() +
  ggspatial::layer_spatial(shp_check, aes(color = as.factor(component)))+
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Troubleshooting

Creation of the igraph object

Creation of the igraph object

We identify the outlet of the river network (the node with the largest upstream area) and we use it as root for building the graph. The custom function create_network() builds the graph based on the river shapefile and the position of confluences and dams.

outlet <- river_net_simplified$NodeID[river_net_simplified$DIST_DN_KM == 0 ]

river_graph <- create_network(network_links, river_net_simplified, outlet)

Creation of the igraph object

We check the created igraph object

river_graph
## IGRAPH 18f0bd9 DN-- 646 645 -- 
## + attr: name (v/n), length (v/n), HYRIV_ID (v/n), NEXT_DOWN (v/n),
## | MAIN_RIV (v/n), LENGTH_KM (v/n), DIST_DN_KM (v/n), DIST_UP_KM (v/n),
## | CATCH_SKM (v/n), UPLAND_SKM (v/n), ENDORHEIC (v/n), DIS_AV_CMS (v/n),
## | ORD_STRA (v/n), ORD_CLAS (v/n), ORD_FLOW (v/n), HYBAS_L12 (v/n), type
## | (e/c), id_links (e/n), id_barrier (e/c), pass_u (e/n), pass_d (e/n)
## + edges from 18f0bd9 (vertex names):
##  [1] 515->516 520->516 519->520 496->520 276->160 153->160 160->164 162->161
##  [9] 161->164 642->640 646->640 641->643 645->643 638->635 640->635 636->629
## [17] 644->629 632->631 639->631 629->628 631->630 630->628 628->627 627->624
## [25] 634->633 633->624 623->626 621->623 596->623 637->626 622->620 625->620
## + ... omitted several edges

Creation of the igraph object

We check the created igraph object

igraph::get.edge.attribute(river_graph) %>% names
## [1] "type"       "id_links"   "id_barrier" "pass_u"     "pass_d"
igraph::get.vertex.attribute(river_graph) %>% names
##  [1] "name"       "length"     "HYRIV_ID"   "NEXT_DOWN"  "MAIN_RIV"  
##  [6] "LENGTH_KM"  "DIST_DN_KM" "DIST_UP_KM" "CATCH_SKM"  "UPLAND_SKM"
## [11] "ENDORHEIC"  "DIS_AV_CMS" "ORD_STRA"   "ORD_CLAS"   "ORD_FLOW"  
## [16] "HYBAS_L12"

Creation of the igraph object

We edit the length attribute of the graph. and we turn the name attribute to character.

V(river_graph)$length <- V(river_graph)$length / 10000
V(river_graph)$name <- as.character(V(river_graph)$name)

Centrality metrics calculation

Centrality metrics calculation

We calculate betweeness and closeness centrality and store the values as nodes attributes

V(river_graph)$btw <- igraph::betweenness(river_graph)
V(river_graph)$cc_dir <- igraph::closeness(river_graph, mode = "out")
V(river_graph)$cc <- igraph::closeness(river_graph, mode = "all")

Centrality metrics calculation

We can plot the centrality metrics. We create a dataframe with all the vertices attributes and join it with the river shapefile for the plotting.

df_river_graph_v <- igraph::as_data_frame(river_graph, what = "vertices") %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(NodeID = as.numeric(name)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(-length)

river_plot <- river_net_simplified %>% 
  left_join(df_river_graph_v)

plot <- ggplot() +
    coord_fixed() + theme_void()+
    ggspatial::layer_spatial(river_plot, aes(color = log10(btw + 1)))+
    scale_color_viridis(name = "Bewteenness (log10)")+
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Centrality metrics calculation

Centrality metrics calculation

Centrality metrics calculation

Thank you for listening!
